channel.title: Alex Sieusahai's Notes
channel.description: Recent content on Alex Sieusahai's Notes

item.title: Exercise 8.1 of Bandit Algorithms
item.description: Statement Show that $$ \sum_{t=1}^n (1/f(t)) \sum_{s=1}^n exp(-s\epsilon^2/2) \leq 5/\epsilon^2, $$ where $f(t) = 1 + t log^2(t)$. Solution This follows the structure of the hints given.

item.title: A First Glance At Explore Then Commit
item.description: The Explore Then Commit bandit algorithm is conceptually very simple and easy to analyze; we first explore all $k$ arms $m$ times, then proceed to exploit the arm with the highest sample mean after that.

item.title: A Markov's Inequality-like Bound For Subgaussian variables
item.description: It’s fairly easy to show that for any $\sigma$-subgaussian random variable $X$ and $\epsilon \geq 0$, we have that $$ \mathbb{P}(X \geq \epsilon) \leq e^{- \frac{\epsilon^2}{2 \sigma^2}} $$ To prove it, just notice that $f(x) = e^{\lambda x}$ is monotonically increasing, and thus we can condlude that for arbitrary $\lambda > 0$: $$ \mathbb{P}(X \geq \epsilon) = \mathbb{P}(e^{\lambda X} \geq e^{\lambda \epsilon}) $$ Notice that we have $e^{\lambda X}$ on the left of our inequality, and recall that for any $\sigma$-subgaussian random variable, we have that $$ \mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda X}] \leq e^{\lambda^2 \sigma^2 / 2} $$ We’d like to incorporate that expectation, and we can do so using Markov’s Inequality: $$ \mathbb{P}(e^{\lambda X} \geq e^{\lambda \epsilon}) \leq \mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda X}] e^{-\lambda \epsilon} $$ Let’s apply the definition of subgaussianity and we’re getting pretty close: $$ \mathbb{E}[e^{\lambda X}] e^{-\lambda \epsilon} \leq e^{\lambda^2 \sigma^2 / 2 - \lambda \epsilon} $$ Now, we’re almost there, we just have to be a little clever.

item.title: Obtaining Tail Bounds For (Subgaussian) Random Variables
item.description: This is exercise 5.5 of Lattimore’s Bandit Algorithms. Statement Let $X_1, X_2, …, X_n$ be a sequence of iid. random variables with mean $\mu$, variance $\sigma^2$ and bounded third absolute moment, $\rho = \mathbb{E}[|X_1 - \mu|^3] < \infty$.

item.title: About Me
item.description: I’m a fourth year undergraduate at the University of Alberta, studying mathematics and computing science. I’ve previously had internships at Jane Street and LinkedIn.

item.title: Markov's Inequality
item.description: Sometimes, it might be useful for us to bound the probability of some random variable $X$ being bigger than some number $\epsilon$.

item.title: The Expectation of Subgaussian Random Variables
item.description: This is a portion of exercise 5.7 in Lattimore’s Bandit Algorithms. The rest of the results are not too hard to prove, but I found this portion of exercise 5.

item.title: Variance Of Average
item.description: This is exercise 5.1 from Lattimore’s Bandit Algorithms. Statement Let $X_1, X_2, …, X_n$ be a sequence of iid. random variables with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2 < \infty$.
